incorrects.description: Student placed tiles but did not form a valid rectangle on first attempt

steps.text: Not quite. A rectangle has rows of equal length.

incorrects.description: Student placed tiles but did not form a valid rectangle on second attempt

params.description: Grid shows example rectangle outline with 3 rows of 4 tiles each.

steps.text: Look at this example: 3 rows, 4 tiles in each row. Every row has the same number. Now use your 12 tiles to make rows that match.

incorrects.description: Student placed tiles but did not form a valid rectangle. System models a valid arrangement.

params.description: System arranges 12 tiles into 3 rows of 4, demonstrating equal row lengths.

steps.text: Let me show you. I'm placing 12 tiles in 3 rows. Each row has 4 tiles. Count them: row 1 has 4, row 2 has 4, row 3 has 4. When every row has the same number, that's a rectangle. The answer is 3 rows of 4.

incorrects.description: Student filled slots incorrectly on first attempt

steps.text: Check your numbers. Count the total tiles, then count the rows.

incorrects.description: Student filled slots incorrectly on second attempt

params.description: One row of the rectangle highlights.

steps.text: The first blank needs the total number of tiles you placed. The second blank needs how many rows you made. Count the rows on your rectangle now.

incorrects.description: Student filled slots incorrectly. System models the correct completion.

params.description: System fills both slots with values matching the student's built rectangle.

steps.text: Here's how to fill it. I count all the tiles: that's 12 total. I count the rows you made: that's the row count. The sentence is: My rectangle has 12 tiles in your-row-count rows. The total goes first, then the row count. That describes what you built.
